name: Ribbon Pakoda
description: Ribbon Pakoda, also known as Seeval or Ola Pakoda, is a crunchy, spicy South Indian snack made from a dough of gram flour and rice flour, pressed into ribbon-like strands and deep-fried. It is a festival favourite during Diwali, easy to make and stays crisp for over 10 days when stored properly. The combination of besan and rice flour gives it a unique texture and addictive crunch.

steps.instruction: Take gram flour, rice flour, cumin seeds, red chilli powder, salt, asafoetida and butter in a large mixing bowl.
Mix well so the butter is evenly incorporated into the flours.
Add water slowly and knead into a soft, slightly sticky dough.
steps.tip: The dough should be soft and slightly sticky — too dry and it will be hard to press; too wet and it will absorb oil.

steps.instruction: Heat oil in a kadai for deep frying on medium-high heat.
Take the ribbon pakoda mould and fit it into an idiyappam press or murukku maker.
steps.tip: Use the ribbon plate (slotted plate with long rectangular holes) for the correct shape.

steps.instruction: Fill the mould with a portion of dough.
Press the dough directly into the hot oil in a circular motion, forming ribbon-like strands.
steps.tip: Press directly into the oil while moving in a circular motion to avoid clumping.

steps.instruction: Fry on medium-high heat until the sizzling sound subsides and the pakoda turns golden.
steps.tip: Fry until the "shhh" sound from the oil stops — this indicates the moisture has evaporated and the pakoda is crisp.

steps.instruction: Flip if needed and fry until evenly crisp.
Cool completely and store in an airtight container for more than 10 days.
steps.tip: Ribbon Pakoda stays crisp for 2 to 3 weeks in an airtight container.

tips: The dough should be soft and slightly sticky — too dry and it will be hard to press; too wet and it will absorb oil.
Use the ribbon plate (slotted plate with long rectangular holes) for the correct shape.
Press directly into the oil while moving in a circular motion to avoid clumping.
Fry until the "shhh" sound from the oil stops — this indicates the moisture has evaporated and the pakoda is crisp.
Do not overcrowd the kadai — fry in batches.
For a peppery version, replace chilli powder with 1 tbsp crushed black pepper.
Ribbon Pakoda stays crisp for 2 to 3 weeks in an airtight container.
